architecture_work.title: Initial Discovery Flow and Alignment Baseline
architecture_work.details:
# Initial Discovery Flow and Alignment Baseline

## Problem Statement

The baseline assessment identifies that the repository has strong foundational assets, including operational guidance, CAS skill definitions, prompt contracts, and knowledge templates. However, governance evidence has not yet been fully established from those assets.

The current state is template-ready rather than operationally governed. Architecture Work, Alignment Reports, Architecture Evolution Maps, and Architecture Intent evidence need to be produced through the lifecycle rather than remaining implicit in templates and guidance.

## Why This Matters

Without an executed discovery and alignment baseline, CAS cannot reliably distinguish intended architecture from observed architecture. Drift detection, governance readiness assessment, and lifecycle progression all depend on populated governance evidence.

This gap affects governance maturity because downstream artifacts such as AEMs and AIDs need source Architecture Work and alignment evidence to summarize.

## Expected Outcomes

The desired future state is an initialized governance baseline for `ideas-to-life-cas` that records the first cycle of discovery, Architecture Work, and alignment evidence.

Success indicators include populated Architecture Work, alignment evidence that can be summarized by governance artifacts, and clear traceability from baseline discovery findings into the architecture backlog.

## Dependencies

- Baseline Discovery
- Architecture Work Registry
- Alignment Engine
- Architecture Evolution Maps
- Architecture Intent Documents

## Discovery Evidence

The baseline assessment states that governance templates are well-defined but populated instances are absent from the provided asset list, indicating a pre-execution or bootstrap state.

It also identifies the candidate to execute the initial discovery flow to generate first Architecture Work and Alignment Report.

## Recommended Next Step

Run the initial discovery-to-alignment lifecycle for `ideas-to-life-cas` and use the resulting evidence to establish the first governed architecture baseline.

architecture_work.origin_ref: CAS Future Vision sections 2, 3.1, 4.1, and 5 Scenario 1
architecture_work.title: Business Intent Integration and Traceability
architecture_work.details:
# Business Intent Integration and Traceability

## Vision Driver

The future vision expands CAS from a decision-driven architecture system into an Architecture Intelligence Platform that connects business intent, architecture, code, and decisions. It specifically calls for ingestion of business requirements, business cases, portfolio definitions, and strategic objectives, then tracing business requirements through ADRs, patterns, and code.

## Current State

CAS has an emerging Architecture Work Registry and a knowledge layer for architecture work, decisions, patterns, maps, and intent. The current diagrams show business stakeholders providing intent and requirements to CAS, but the project does not yet define a governed capability for extracting business intent signals or linking those signals through the architecture knowledge model.

## Desired Future State

CAS should represent business intent as governed architecture knowledge that can be traced to patterns, ADRs, architecture work, implementation structure, and runtime behavior. Architects should be able to ask which architectural decisions support a business capability and receive evidence-backed traceability.

## Evolution Gap

The gap is a missing business-intent model, ingestion workflow, and traceability relationship contract. Current knowledge artifacts can hold architecture evidence, but they do not yet provide a first-class path from business inputs to architecture decisions and downstream implementation evidence.

## Why This Matters

Business intent integration gives CAS strategic relevance beyond architecture documentation. Architecturally, it anchors decisions in business drivers. From a governance perspective, it improves decision justification and review context. Operationally, it makes change impact easier to reason about across business, architecture, and implementation layers.

## Expected Outcomes

- Business intent signals become governed architecture inputs.
- Architecture Work and ADRs can reference business drivers explicitly.
- Traceability queries can connect business requirements to patterns, decisions, and code evidence.
- Governance review gains clearer evidence for why architectural choices matter.

## Dependencies

- Architecture Work Registry
- Knowledge Provider
- ADR lifecycle
- Pattern relationship model
- Future code intelligence capability

## Vision Evidence

The vision asks how to connect business intent to architecture and implementation, defines Business Layer Integration as a capability domain, and identifies end-to-end traceability from business to decision, pattern, code, and runtime behavior as a cross-cutting capability.

## Recommended Next Step

Define the minimal business intent knowledge model and identify the first governed ingestion workflow that can convert business artifacts into Architecture Work without introducing implementation design.

architecture_work.title: Code Intelligence and Architecture Alignment
architecture_work.details:
# Code Intelligence and Architecture Alignment

## Vision Driver

The future vision calls for CAS to move from pattern extraction toward full code understanding and continuous alignment between intended architecture and implementation reality.

## Current State

The current execution sequence and orchestration diagrams show code repositories, CI, rule context loading, alignment analysis, and architecture work generation. These flows establish a path for operational governance, but code intelligence remains primarily represented as future structural analysis, deterministic extraction, and alignment signals rather than a mature capability.

## Desired Future State

CAS should understand codebase structure, identify boundaries and dependencies, map implementation evidence to patterns and architecture intent, and detect where code deviates from AID, ADRs, or governed patterns. The capability should support both prevention and detection of architectural drift.

## Evolution Gap

The gap is a governed code intelligence model that can translate repository structure into architecture evidence. Current workflows can run and persist governance artifacts, but they do not yet provide a complete architectural representation of modules, dependencies, boundaries, drift, and fit/gap recommendations.

## Why This Matters

Code intelligence is foundational to closing the loop between architecture intent and delivery. Architecturally, it grounds AID and AEM claims in implementation evidence. Governance improves because drift becomes visible earlier. Operationally, teams can implement features and refactors with stronger architectural context.

## Expected Outcomes

- Code structure is represented as governed architecture evidence.
- Alignment checks compare AID, ADRs, patterns, and code reality.
- Drift findings produce Architecture Work rather than remaining isolated reports.
- CI and delivery workflows gain evidence-backed prevention and detection signals.

## Dependencies

- Architecture Work Registry
- GovernanceOps
- AID and AEM lifecycle
- Rule context loading
- CI/CD validation model

## Vision Evidence

The vision identifies code structural analysis, architectural boundary detection, code-to-pattern mapping, AID-vs-code comparison, and automated fit/gap analysis as required future capabilities.

## Recommended Next Step

Define the first code intelligence evidence model and the governance boundary for how code-derived findings become Architecture Work, alignment findings, or CI validation signals.

architecture_work.title: Initial ADR for the Core Orchestration Pipeline
architecture_work.details:
# Initial ADR for the Core Orchestration Pipeline

## Problem Statement

The baseline assessment identifies orchestration flows and alignment engines as structurally ready, but it does not identify a populated Architecture Decision Record capturing the core orchestration pipeline as a governed architectural decision.

The current state leaves a foundational runtime and governance choice implicit in repository structure, prompt contracts, and operational documentation.

## Why This Matters

The orchestration pipeline is a central architectural capability for CAS. If its role is not captured as an ADR, future changes may alter the pipeline without a clear decision record, rationale, or governance trail.

This affects governance because architecture evolution depends on stable decision provenance for core runtime and workflow boundaries.

## Expected Outcomes

The desired future state is a populated ADR that records the core orchestration pipeline decision, explains why CAS uses the pipeline as a governing execution model, and establishes traceability to the baseline assessment.

Success indicators include a decision record with clear status, rationale, affected components, and links to related Architecture Work and governance artifacts.

## Dependencies

- Governance Foundation
- Architecture Decision Records
- Core orchestration pipeline
- Alignment Engine

## Discovery Evidence

The baseline assessment identifies the candidate to create an initial Architecture Decision Record for the core orchestration pipeline.

It also concludes that orchestration flows and alignment engines are structurally ready but require actual architecture work and decision records to mature.

## Recommended Next Step

Promote the core orchestration pipeline decision into an ADR candidate and route it through the project governance lifecycle.

architecture_work.title: Operational Intelligence Loop for ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps
architecture_work.details:
# Operational Intelligence Loop for ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps

## Vision Driver

The future vision positions ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps as continuously participating operational capabilities that convert conversations, implementation activity, architecture work, and governance signals into architectural intelligence.

## Current State

The project has established Governance Foundation v1, governance-aware orchestration diagrams, and architecture work for CI skill enforcement. The orchestration flow also includes operational intelligence as a participant. However, operational intelligence is not yet defined as a complete lifecycle that consistently evaluates signals, recommends architecture evolution, and feeds governance refinement.

## Desired Future State

CAS should continuously evaluate alignment between intent, implementation, patterns, decisions, and system state. It should surface governance drift, emerging patterns, capability evolution, and prioritized architecture work while keeping human governance authoritative.

## Evolution Gap

The gap is a defined operational intelligence loop with clear signal sources, reasoning responsibilities, governance outputs, lifecycle states, and trust calibration. Current governance participation exists, but the future operating model requires a repeatable prevent, detect, and feedback loop.

## Why This Matters

ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps turn governance from periodic review into lightweight participation in delivery. Architecturally, this improves the system's ability to reason about itself. Governance becomes adaptive because execution evidence can refine patterns and decisions. Operationally, teams receive earlier and more relevant architecture guidance.

## Expected Outcomes

- Operational signals consistently produce architecture intelligence.
- Governance drift and emerging patterns are routed into Architecture Work.
- Reasoning loops support prioritization of architecture evolution.
- Human review remains the authority for accepting decisions and governance changes.

## Dependencies

- Governance Foundation
- Architecture Work Registry
- CI/CD governance signals
- Alignment Engine
- Operational intelligence artifact model

## Vision Evidence

The vision describes ReasoningOps and GovernanceOps as capabilities for continuous evaluation, architecture evolution recommendations, governance drift detection, pattern refinement, trust calibration, and operational participation throughout delivery.

## Recommended Next Step

Define the operational intelligence artifact contract and map how prevent, detect, and feedback signals flow into Architecture Work, ADRs, pattern updates, and alignment reports.

# Governance Portal Implementation

architecture_work.title: Architecture Knowledge Graph Development
architecture_work.details:
# Architecture Knowledge Graph Development

## Summary

Development of an Architecture Knowledge Graph for cross-project relationship modeling and lineage analysis.

## Details

The vision identifies a need for an 'Architecture Intelligence Layer'. This work item involves transitioning from isolated project-level artifacts to a unified graph-based representation of architectural knowledge, enabling complex queries across projects and time.

## Decision

Proposed: Adopt a graph-based schema for representing relationships between ADRs, Patterns, Signals, and Code artifacts.

## Rationale

Current artifacts are siloed. A Knowledge Graph enables the system to reason about the impact of a pattern change in one project on the reliability of another, fulfilling the 'Continuous Intelligence' vision.

## Implementation Plan

1. Define the Graph Schema (Nodes: Decision, Pattern, Signal, Artifact). 2. Implement Knowledge Providers for graph ingestion. 3. Develop query interfaces for the Alignment Engine.

## Alternatives Considered

['Relational database storage (rejected due to the complexity of recursive relationship traversal)', 'Enhanced flat-file indexing (rejected as insufficient for multi-hop lineage analysis)']

## Risks

['Complexity of maintaining graph consistency across distributed projects', 'Performance overhead of graph traversal during runtime reasoning']

## Expected Outcomes

Ability to perform cross-project impact analysis; visual lineage of architectural decisions; improved signal-to-pattern promotion accuracy.

## Dependencies

Knowledge Provider Abstraction, Architecture Evolution Map

## Recommended Next Step

Draft the initial graph schema for Decision-Pattern-Signal relationships.

architecture_work.title: Business Intent Integration
architecture_work.details:
# Business Intent Integration

## Summary

Integration of Business Intent into the architectural reasoning loop to ensure strategic alignment.

## Details

This work item bridges the gap between high-level business vision and architectural execution. It involves creating a formal mechanism for the system to ingest 'Business Intent' artifacts and use them as constraints during the 'Analyse' phase of the governance loop.

## Decision

Proposed: Formalise the 'Business Intent' artifact schema and integrate it as a mandatory input for the Alignment Engine.

## Rationale

The vision emphasizes 'connecting business intent to code reality'. Without this integration, architectural governance remains focused on technical consistency rather than strategic value.

## Implementation Plan

1. Define the Business Intent Schema. 2. Update the Alignment Engine to weight 'Intent Alignment' as a high-severity metric. 3. Create a 'VisionToIntent' workflow to automate artifact generation.

## Alternatives Considered

['Manual mapping of intent to architecture (rejected as non-scalable)', 'Treating intent as purely narrative documentation (rejected as it prevents automated alignment validation)']

## Risks

['Ambiguity in business intent could lead to conflicting architectural constraints', "Potential for 'intent drift' where business goals change faster than architecture can evolve"]

## Expected Outcomes

Traceability from code to business value; automated detection of 'Strategic Drift'; improved prioritisation of Architecture Work based on business impact.

## Dependencies

Alignment Engine, Vision Artifacts

## Recommended Next Step

Conduct a pilot mapping of the 'CAS 2026 Vision' to the proposed Business Intent Schema.

architecture_work.title: External Ecosystem and Ingestion Framework
architecture_work.details:
# External Ecosystem and Ingestion Framework

## Vision Driver

The future vision positions CAS within enterprise architecture ecosystems and calls for a plugin or ingestion framework for BRD parsers, code analyzers, external system connectors, and knowledge platform integrations.

## Current State

CAS currently has local knowledge artifacts, prompt-based workflows, runner automation, and diagrams showing LLM providers, CI/CD, code repositories, and runtime systems as adjacent participants. External enterprise architecture tools, source-control integrations beyond local repository workflows, and knowledge-platform synchronization remain future-facing.

## Desired Future State

CAS should ingest and synchronize architecture knowledge across enterprise architecture management tools, knowledge platforms, source control systems, and CI/CD pipelines. New input types should follow a standard ingestion interface so external evidence can become governed Architecture Work, ADRs, patterns, maps, and intent.

## Evolution Gap

The gap is a stable integration boundary and provider model for external architecture evidence. Current workflows are mostly file and prompt oriented; the future state requires governed connectors, synchronization rules, provenance handling, and lifecycle integration for external systems.

## Why This Matters

External ecosystem integration prevents CAS from becoming an isolated repository-local architecture tool. Architecturally, it establishes clean boundaries for external providers. Governance improves because decisions and patterns can synchronize across systems. Operationally, teams can bring existing enterprise evidence into CAS without manual duplication.

## Expected Outcomes

- External inputs follow a standard ingestion and provenance model.
- Enterprise tools and knowledge platforms can contribute governed architecture evidence.
- CAS can export validated decisions and patterns to external repositories.
- Synchronization responsibilities are explicit and governable.

## Dependencies

- Knowledge Provider
- Business Intent Integration
- Architecture Work Registry
- Provenance and source tracking conventions
- Future connector contracts

## Vision Evidence

The vision identifies enterprise architecture management tools, knowledge platforms, source control systems, CI/CD pipelines, external artifact ingestion, decision synchronization, and plugin-based input interfaces as target capabilities.

## Recommended Next Step

Define the external ingestion provider contract and governance rules for provenance, synchronization direction, conflict handling, and Architecture Work creation.

architecture_work.title: Continuous Architecture Governance Evolution
architecture_work.details:
# Continuous Architecture Governance Evolution

## Summary

The CAS platform is evolving from a documentation and manual governance model into a Continuous Architecture Governance platform. This work captures the evolution of the Governance Portal, Pattern Governance Lifecycle, Workflow Execution Model, Governance Intelligence/Metrics, Compliance Engine, and the strategic vision for a Runtime-native Governance layer.

## Details

Covers Governance Portal evolution (Sprints 1-3), Pattern Governance lifecycle institutionalization, Workflow Execution Model compliance, Governance Intelligence domain modeling, Traceable Metrics, Artifact Reference Resolver, Rules vs Policies decision, Governance Compliance & Exception framework, Observability fixes, Governance Action Framework design, and Strategic Vision for Continuous Governance Runtime.

## Decision

Institutionalize a formal Governance Lifecycle (Proposal -> Promotion Recommendation -> Promotion Decision -> ADR -> Pattern). Adopt a unified Workflow Execution Model where CAS Runner orchestrates and workflows reason. Select independent workflows over a unified generator for Architecture Work. Establish Rules as the authoritative governance constraint source over separate policies. Introduce a Governance Action Framework for state changes.

## Rationale

Manual execution caused intelligence staleness and architecture drift. A standardized execution model ensures consistency. Formalizing the lifecycle prevents governance state leakage into proposal artifacts. Rules provide a flexible, auditable compliance engine. The Action Framework addresses the gap in human approval automation.

## Implementation Plan

1. Complete Workflow Onboarding Program for conversation/discovery/vision-to-aw. 2. Implement Governance Action Engine and CLI/Portal interfaces. 3. Deploy Governance Compliance Engine with CI/CD integration. 4. Develop Traceable Metrics and Artifact Reference Resolver. 5. Evolve Portal to Architecture Intelligence Portal. 6. Future: Implement Continuous Pattern Intelligence and Portfolio Intelligence.

## Alternatives Considered

['Option A (Independent workflows) vs Option B (Unified Architecture Work Generator) -> Selected A for lower risk and validation of execution model.', 'Separate Policy Hierarchy vs Existing Rule Framework -> Selected Rules as authoritative source.', 'Markdown-only artifacts vs Structured JSON/YAML -> Adopted structured schemas for machine consumption.']

## Risks

['Intelligence freshness remains dependent on workflow execution frequency.', 'Data fragmentation across conversations, ADRs, AEMs, and external tools (Jira, GitHub Projects).', 'Manual approval bottlenecks in current governance actions.', 'Potential over-engineering of the Knowledge Graph layer.']

## Expected Outcomes

Reduced architecture drift through continuous governance. Machine-consumable governance artifacts. Runtime-native workflow execution. Improved portal navigation and drill-down capabilities. Clear separation of governance state and actions. Foundation for automated pattern discovery and portfolio intelligence.

## Dependencies

CAS Runner orchestration, Knowledge Provider abstraction, ReasoningExecutionContract, GitHub Actions automation, MkDocs/GitHub Pages portal infrastructure.

## Recommended Next Step

Finalize and onboard remaining prompt-native workflows to the CAS Runner. Implement the Governance Action Framework to automate approval/rejection flows. Begin prototyping the Architecture Knowledge Graph for cross-project intelligence.

architecture_work.title: Automated Detect-Analyse-Execute Loop for Governance
architecture_work.details:
# Automated Detect-Analyse-Execute Loop for Governance

## Summary

Establishment of an automated 'Detect-Analyse-Execute' loop to operationalise architectural governance.

## Details

This work item focuses on moving from manual alignment reporting to a closed-loop governance system. It involves integrating the Alignment Engine with CI/CD pipelines to automatically detect drift, analyse impact using the CAS reasoning model, and propose or execute remediation workflows.

## Decision

Proposed: Implement a runtime-native governance controller that orchestrates the detection and analysis phases without human intervention for non-breaking drift.

## Rationale

The current governance velocity ratio (15:1) indicates that manual review is a bottleneck. Automation of the governance loop is required to maintain architectural integrity at scale.

## Implementation Plan

1. Define CI-based triggers for alignment checks. 2. Develop the 'Analyse' workflow to consume alignment reports. 3. Integrate with the Architecture Work Registry to auto-generate 'proposed' items for detected drift.

## Alternatives Considered

['Maintain manual review cycles (rejected due to velocity bottleneck)', 'Purely deterministic rule enforcement (rejected as it lacks the reasoning capability required for complex patterns)']

## Risks

['High volume of automated work items could overwhelm the registry', 'False positives in drift detection could lead to unnecessary refactoring']

## Expected Outcomes

Reduction in governance velocity bottleneck; 100% visibility of architectural drift in real-time; automated provenance for all governance-driven changes.

## Dependencies

Alignment Engine, Architecture Work Registry, CAS Workflow Execution Model

## Recommended Next Step

Define the semantic contract for 'Automated Remediation' signals within the Alignment Engine.

architecture_work.title: Project-Specific Knowledge Layer Template Validation
architecture_work.details:
# Project-Specific Knowledge Layer Template Validation

## Problem Statement

The baseline assessment identifies well-defined knowledge layer templates for ADRs, AEMs, AIDs, and Architecture Work, but it also identifies a need to validate and populate those templates with project-specific patterns and rules.

The current state provides reusable governance structure, but the project-specific governance substrate needs evidence that it reflects the actual architecture constraints of `ideas-to-life-cas`.

## Why This Matters

Template readiness is not the same as project governance readiness. CAS needs populated and validated knowledge artifacts so generated outputs remain grounded in the project architecture rather than generic template structure.

This affects architecture quality because unvalidated templates can create a false sense of governance maturity while leaving project-specific constraints implicit.

## Expected Outcomes

The desired future state is a validated project knowledge layer where templates, patterns, rules, and generated artifacts are populated with `ideas-to-life-cas` specific architectural constraints.

Success indicators include project-specific knowledge artifacts, validated frontmatter contracts, and clear relationships between templates, patterns, rules, and generated governance evidence.

## Dependencies

- Knowledge Provider
- Governance Foundation
- Architecture Work Registry
- Project-specific patterns and rules

## Discovery Evidence

The baseline assessment identifies the candidate to validate and populate knowledge layer templates with project-specific patterns and rules.

It also notes that the knowledge layer templates enforce strict frontmatter and schema contracts supporting deterministic derivation.

## Recommended Next Step

Review the project knowledge templates against current `ideas-to-life-cas` architecture constraints and populate the first project-specific governance artifacts needed for deterministic lifecycle execution.

architecture_work.title: Interactive Architecture Intelligence Maps and Knowledge Graph
architecture_work.details:
# Interactive Architecture Intelligence Maps and Knowledge Graph

## Vision Driver

The future vision calls for an interactive Architecture Intelligence Map, decision flow visualization, pattern relationship graph, timeline of architecture evolution, and a future knowledge graph layer for complex queries.

## Current State

CAS has static diagram artifacts, architecture maps, intent documents, and relationship-bearing frontmatter. These provide architectural visibility, but exploration remains document-centric rather than graph-centric or interactive.

## Desired Future State

CAS should provide an explorable architecture intelligence layer where architects can navigate decisions, patterns, architecture work, maps, intent, code evidence, and business intent as connected knowledge. The same relationship model should support visual exploration and structured queries.

## Evolution Gap

The gap is a first-class relationship graph model and interaction layer. Current artifacts contain relationships, but they are not yet normalized into a governed graph that can power visualization, timelines, impact analysis, or cross-project queries.

## Why This Matters

Interactive architecture intelligence makes CAS usable as an exploration and reasoning platform, not only a document generator. Architecturally, it reveals relationships and change impact. Governance improves because decision lineage and pattern evolution become easier to inspect. Operationally, it reduces the cost of understanding why the system evolved.

## Expected Outcomes

- CAS knowledge relationships are normalized into a graph-ready model.
- Architects can explore decision flow, pattern relationships, and evolution timelines.
- Cross-artifact and cross-project queries become possible.
- Visualization reflects governed source artifacts rather than becoming a separate source of truth.

## Dependencies

- Knowledge Provider
- Architecture Work Registry
- Business Intent Integration
- Code Intelligence
- Relationship and provenance conventions

## Vision Evidence

The vision identifies AIM, decision flow visualization, pattern relationship graphs, evolution timelines, and knowledge graph queries as future capabilities for making architecture explorable and interactive.

## Recommended Next Step

Define the governed relationship model for Architecture Work, ADRs, patterns, AEM, AID, diagrams, business intent, and code evidence before selecting visualization or graph storage mechanisms.

architecture_work.title: Architecture Work Registry as Authoritative Architectural Backlog
architecture_work.details:
# Architecture Work Registry as Authoritative Architectural Backlog

## 1. Decisions

### Decision

Establish Architecture Work as the authoritative architectural change backlog for CAS.

### Rationale

AEM regeneration currently discards manually introduced planning information. Architectural evolution should be persisted in Architecture Work artifacts and summarized by downstream governance artifacts.

Decision Type: adoption

Decision Status: proposed

---

## 2. Prompt Changes

### conversation-to-architecture-work.v1.md

Change:

- Added initiative classification
- Added lifecycle_state classification
- Added related_work support
- Added architecture work lifecycle heuristics

Reason:

Improve Architecture Work traceability, deduplication, and evolution tracking.

### architecture_work_template.md

Change:

- Added initiative
- Added category
- Added related_work
- Added lifecycle_state

Reason:

Align Architecture Work artifacts with the emerging Architecture Work Registry model.

---

## 3. Pattern Operations

### Refined Pattern

Architecture Work Registry

Description:

Architecture Work becomes the authoritative source of architectural change while AEM becomes a generated architectural dashboard.

Candidate Signal: Regenerated Artifacts Should Not Own Planning State

Insight:

Information repeatedly lost through artifact regeneration should be persisted in authoritative source artifacts rather than generated views.

Candidate Signal: Governance Artifacts Prefer Derived State

Insight:

Governance outputs should summarize architecture state rather than become the source of truth themselves.

---

## 4. Structural Changes

### Knowledge Layer Evolution

Introduced:

text Architecture Work Registry         ↓ AEM         ↓ AID         ↓ Alignment 

### Frontmatter Evolution

Added support for:

- initiative grouping
- lifecycle classification
- work correlation
- architecture backlog ownership

---

## 5. System Evolution

### New Architecture Work Producers

Introduced:

text Conversation         ↓ Architecture Work  Discovery         ↓ Architecture Work  Vision         ↓ Architecture Work  Alignment         ↓ Architecture Work 

### Governance Evolution

Architecture Work elevated from supporting artifact to authoritative governance planning layer.

### Lifecycle Evolution

Architecture Work now supports:

- new
- active
- completed
- superseded

classification across regeneration cycles.

---

## 6. Intent & Direction

### Architecture Work Registry

Establish a persistent architecture backlog capable of receiving work from:

- conversations
- discovery findings
- vision artifacts
- alignment findings

### Future Direction

Potential future integration with:

- GitHub Projects
- GitHub Issues
- Jira
- Azure Boards

while maintaining Architecture Work as the governance-centric architectural planning layer.

---

## 7. Roadmap Decisions

### Priority Order Accepted

1. Discovery → Architecture Work
2. Vision → Architecture Work
3. Alignment → Architecture Work

Deferred:

- GitHub/Jira synchronization
- AEM delta extraction
- Governance review extraction

Reason:

Validate Architecture Work Registry operating model before introducing additional producers.

---

## 8. Open Questions

- Should Architecture Work gain stable work identifiers beyond generated IDs?
- Should initiatives become first-class artifacts?
- How should external backlog systems synchronize with Architecture Work?
- Should completed Architecture Work automatically influence AEM lifecycle classification?

---

## 9. Architecture Work Classification

Category:

- governance

Lifecycle State:

- active

Related Work:

- none currently identified

Initiative:

- Architecture Work Registry

### Classification Rationale

The capability is beyond initial proposal and is actively being operationalized through:

- template evolution
- ingestion workflow creation
- governance model alignment

Implementation is not yet fully complete, therefore classification remains:

text active 

rather than:

text completed

architecture_work.title: Automated CI Validation for CAS Skill Enforcement
architecture_work.details:
# Automated CI Validation for CAS Skill Enforcement

## Problem Statement

The baseline assessment identifies mandatory CAS skill usage as an architectural governance concern and proposes automated CI validation for CAS skill enforcement.

The current state depends on contributor and agent discipline to preserve the governed workflow, but discovery indicates an opportunity to make enforcement visible and repeatable through automation.

## Why This Matters

CAS skill enforcement protects the repository from architectural drift by ensuring changes pass through governed workflows. Without automation, enforcement can become inconsistent across contributors, agents, and pull requests.

This affects governance operations because architectural guardrails should be verifiable as part of the delivery lifecycle rather than relying only on manual review.

## Expected Outcomes

The desired future state is CI validation that makes CAS skill enforcement observable during repository changes.

Success indicators include an automated check tied to governed workflow expectations, clear failure output for non-compliant changes, and reduced reliance on manual detection of skill bypasses.

## Dependencies

- GovernanceOps
- CAS Skill Enforcement
- CI/CD workflow governance
- Architecture Work Registry

## Discovery Evidence

The baseline assessment identifies the candidate to implement automated CI validation for CAS skill enforcement.

It states that this would prevent architectural drift by ensuring all code changes pass through mandatory CAS skills.

## Recommended Next Step

Define the CI-level governance signal for CAS skill enforcement and route the proposed automation through the repository's managed steering and validation workflow.
